note: The gleaming steel 'dream boat' sculpture on the waterfront; the Saebraut promenade is wide, paved, step-free and snow-cleared in winter, so fully wheelchair/stroller accessible, with benches and Faxafloi Bay + Mt Esja views. Free, open 24/7.

Iceland's stunning glass-faceted concert/conference hall on the harbour; wheelchair accessible throughout with lifts, accessible toilets and hearing loops, plus 30-min accessible tours in English. Free to wander the foyer.

The soaring landmark church; the building itself is accessible (and the tower has a lift for the view), but Skolavordustigur, the pretty street leading up to it, is uphill - a push may be needed. Einar Jonsson sculpture garden nearby is accessible.

Iceland's famous geothermal spa (45 min from the city); genuinely accessible - accessible changing rooms and a ramp/lift into the milky water, known for being very accommodating. Book ahead. A must-do.

The thundering double-tiered waterfall; a paved path from the car park leads to an accessible viewing deck over the falls (some paths are a bit steep). One of the most accessible natural wonders in Iceland.
The geothermal field where Strokkur erupts every few minutes; the main viewing area has paved accessible paths and there's a cosy restaurant. Reliable and easy - a Golden Circle highlight.

UNESCO rift-valley national park (site of the first parliament, tectonic plates meeting); much of it uses wooden-decking/dense-gravel paths that are largely accessible, but the awkward parking layout and some terrain make parts tricky. Book an accessible-tour operator.
Reykjavik's main shopping/dining street; thanks to the 'Ramping Up Iceland' campaign (1500+ new ramps), almost every business is now wheelchair accessible. Lively and easy to browse.
Museum of Iceland's natural wonders (ice cave, planetarium) under a glass dome with a 360 observation deck; accessible via lifts, a great family + rainy-day option with city panoramas.

Boat tours from the Old Harbour; the path there is narrow and winding and boarding vessels varies by operator - contact ahead for accessible boats. Weather-dependent and can be chilly, but a memorable wildlife trip.
